title: Dead regex no-op in archive timestamp construction
description: The timestamp builder chains two .replace() calls; the second one replaces '.' with '.' — a pure no-op that does nothing to the string. Looks like a leftover from a different filesystem-safety scheme that never landed.
impact: Reader of the code has to pause and check whether the dot-to-dot rewrite has a special meaning (e.g. regex escaping nuance). It does not. Pure noise on the hot path of the corruption branch.
recommendation: Delete the second .replace(/\\./g, '.') call. The .replace(/:/g, '-') alone is sufficient to make the ISO-8601 string filesystem-safe.

title: Unused parameter `_statePath` on archiveCorruptFile
description: archiveCorruptFile takes (stateDir, _statePath, content) but never reads _statePath. The underscore prefix flags 'intentionally unused', but the parameter itself is also unused at every call site's contract — every caller passes statePath only because the signature demands it.
impact: Three call sites pass an argument that the function ignores. Future readers may assume the function must inspect or mutate the canonical statePath (it does not, by design — that is a load-bearing property per A4). Removing the parameter makes the 'archive does not touch canonical' invariant visible at the type level.
recommendation: Drop the _statePath parameter from archiveCorruptFile and update the three call sites in loadState. The function is logically (stateDir, content) → archivePath.

title: bootHarness accepts BootOptions.projectRoot but never uses it
description: bootHarness receives `_options: BootOptions` containing a required `projectRoot` field, but the body never reads it — projectRoot flows in per-call via callTool's args. The parameter exists only because cycle 1's contract forced it through, and the rewrite kept it as dead weight.
impact: Callers must construct a BootOptions value with a projectRoot they know will be ignored, then pass projectRoot a second time on every callTool invocation. Confusing for downstream cycles (cycle 4 will boot the harness many times) and misleading at the type seam — looks like the harness is project-scoped when it is in fact stateless.
recommendation: Either drop the BootOptions parameter entirely (`bootHarness(): Promise<HarnessInstance>`), or repurpose it to genuinely scope the harness (e.g. default projectRoot baked into the returned callTool). The current shape is the worst of both worlds.

title: Belt-and-suspenders uniqueness in saveState tmp filename
description: The tmp file path combines Date.now() AND Math.random().toString(36).slice(2) for uniqueness. Either alone is sufficient for the contract's same-process uniqueness requirement (saveState is sequential per-project; even concurrent saves on the same project root almost never share a millisecond, and Math.random gives ~52 bits of entropy on its own).
impact: Minor code noise — two uniqueness sources where one would do. Not a bug, but a reader has to mentally justify why both are needed and conclude 'they aren't'.
recommendation: Pick one. `state.tmp-${Math.random().toString(36).slice(2)}.json` is the simplest sufficient form. Keep Date.now() only if you want the tmp file to be human-sortable for debugging.

title: Module-global archiveCounter is heavier than the test requirement needs
description: A module-level mutable counter (`let archiveCounter = 0`) is incremented on every corruption to disambiguate same-millisecond archives. The ISO-8601 timestamp already includes milliseconds, and T-050's two corruptions are separated by a full archive write + JS event loop turn — empirically they almost always land on different milliseconds. The counter is belt-and-suspenders against a near-impossible collision, at the cost of a module-scope side-effect channel that resets only on process restart.
impact: Adds a hidden piece of mutable state to a file otherwise written as pure functions over (projectRoot, fs). Counter monotonically grows for the life of the process; long-running servers see archive names like `state.corrupt-...-12345.json` after enough corruptions, which leaks process lifetime info into filenames.
recommendation: Either drop the counter entirely and rely on the millisecond timestamp (the test passes empirically because the two reads aren't synchronous within a ms), or replace with `process.hrtime.bigint()` appended to the suffix — same uniqueness guarantee without module-level mutation.
evidence:
// Monotonic counter to ensure uniqueness within the same millisecond
let archiveCounter = 0;

title: Hand-rolled validateState duplicates pattern that zod could express in ~10 lines
description: validateState is ~64 lines of hand-rolled type-narrowing checks returning {ok, value|error}. zod is already a runtime dependency (used in mcp/server/src/index.ts for tool input schema). A z.object({...}) declaration plus a thin .safeParse wrapper would cover every assertion the tests check (T-067..T-074) in roughly a third of the lines, with better error messages for free.
impact: Cycle 1 chose the same hand-rolled approach in schemas/path.ts and schemas/phases.ts, so this is a consistency choice, not a one-off. Worth flagging because cycle 4's State will grow (ladder rung shape, history event enum, personalization narrowing) and the hand-rolled validator scales linearly while zod scales sublinearly.
recommendation: Not for this cycle — consistency with the existing schemas/ pattern is the higher value today. Flag for cycle 4 when State validation gets stricter: at that point convert all three schemas/*.ts files to zod in one pass.

title: Warnings array constructed twice in runStart
description: Both branches of the outputStyleOk gate build their own `warnings` local from `[...registry.warnings]`. The early-return path also conditionally unshifts the styleResult warning. The post-gate path builds a fresh copy and pushes state warnings. Two near-identical constructions of the same array; the only divergence is which side gets the styleResult warning unshifted.
impact: Minor duplication. A future maintainer adding (say) a third warning source will have to remember to update both branches. Not a bug; a refactor opportunity.
recommendation: Build `warnings` once at the top from `[...registry.warnings]`, push the styleResult warning if present, then early-return on !styleResult.ok carrying that array. The state-switch then pushes onto the same array. Removes one local declaration and one .unshift call.

title: SDK re-export through index.ts is pragmatic but couples library to harness
description: index.ts re-exports `McpServer`, `Client`, and `InMemoryTransport` from @modelcontextprotocol/sdk specifically to give scripts/e2e/harness.ts a single import surface across the workspace boundary. The library is pulling its weight (per synthesis-notes.md, this is the load-bearing distinction between worker-2 and worker-1's harness.ts split), but it leaks SDK transport classes into the public surface of the server module — anything that imports `@sui-deepbook-course/mcp-server` now sees Client and InMemoryTransport in its namespace.
impact: Forward concern: when cycle 3+ adds publishing or a third-party consumer of the server package, those re-exports become public API surface that must be maintained. Worker-1's alternative (a dedicated mcp/server/src/harness.ts that the e2e script delegates to) keeps the seam internal.
recommendation: Acceptable for now — the synthesis correctly picked the simpler tree. Revisit before any package-publish step: move the harness boot logic into mcp/server/src/harness.ts (matching worker-1's shape) and have scripts/e2e/harness.ts import only that, removing the SDK re-exports from index.ts.

title: loadState's archive call site duplicates the same five lines three times
description: Three branches in loadState build the same shape: call archiveCorruptFile, return { kind: 'corrupt', archivedTo, message: `... archived original to ${archivedTo}.` }. The only thing that differs between them is the reason fragment ('invalid JSON' vs 'missing schema_version' vs 'schema validation failed: ...').
impact: Three near-identical 5-line blocks. A future reviewer adding a fourth corruption trigger (say, schema_version is negative) will copy-paste a fourth one. Cycle 4 is likely to add at least one more validator (ladder rung shape, cursor existence inside ladder).
recommendation: Extract a small helper: `async function classifyCorrupt(stateDir, raw, reason): Promise<LoadStateResult>` that does the archive and returns the corrupt envelope. The three branches collapse to one-liners passing the reason fragment.

title: validateState rebuilds the input object field-by-field instead of returning the validated reference
description: On the success path, validateState constructs a brand-new object copying every field from the validated input — schema_version, selected_path, personalization, cursor (rebuilt from cursor.phase_id and cursor.spot_id only), ladder, history. Since the validator has already proven the shape matches `State`, returning `{ ok: true, value: obj as unknown as State }` would be equivalent and shorter. The hand-rebuilt cursor in particular silently drops any extra properties on cursor (a subtle behavior change vs the rest of the object, which gets cast wholesale).
impact: Asymmetric handling: top-level extra fields would survive the cast (because `obj` is reused), but extra fields on `cursor` are stripped (because cursor is rebuilt). This is either an accidental data-loss bug for forward-compat schemas or an intentional but unstated normalization. Either way it is surprising. It also adds ~14 lines of allocation-on-the-hot-path code.
recommendation: Pick a policy and apply it consistently. Either (a) cast the validated input wholesale: `return { ok: true, value: obj as unknown as State }`, or (b) document and enforce the strip-extras policy in tests and apply it uniformly to every nested object. Option (a) is the simpler choice and matches how schemas/path.ts handles it.
